title: ProtocolLab Specification Coverage Model
purpose: Describe how versioned source documents, requirement records, scenario/check mappings, named profiles, and optional evidence sidecars satisfy the specification-coverage requirements without changing existing scenario or evidence-report contracts.
design_summary: The public contract adds standalone specification document and requirement records, catalog manifests, scenario-to-requirement mapping documents, and named coverage profiles. Mapping documents reference existing exact scenario and validation check IDs but do not modify Scenario v1. Run Plan v1 may optionally select and hash-pin package-provided coverage inputs, and Test Executor v1 may optionally advertise exact structured-outcome capabilities. Runtime producers may emit a separately versioned coverage-evidence sidecar through the existing artifact path. The sidecar pins catalog, mapping, profile, and scenario-package digests and records per-check outcomes. Existing plans, executors, and reports remain valid when the optional fields or sidecar are absent.
key_components: stable requirement records with RFCXML-derived source-unit addresses
named coverage profiles with explicit denominators
scenario-pack metadata and package-relative mapping snapshots
optional run-plan catalog, mapping, profile, requirement, and check selection
optional executor structured-outcome authority and compatibility discovery
optional per-cell structured validation check outcome artifacts
data_and_state_considerations: Catalog and mapping documents are immutable by version. Review state is explicit. Historical evidence stores IDs, versions, and SHA-256 digests rather than joining to the current catalog. Mapping relationships describe design intent; check outcomes describe execution. Diagnostic and publication qualifiers remain owned by the source report and are never upgraded by the mapping model.
edge_cases_and_constraints: Internet-Drafts are mutable and therefore require an exact revision.
Conditional requirements can be not-applicable without being unsupported.
A negative-validates check can pass by observing required rejection or prevention rather than successful traffic.
An exercises or observes relationship cannot produce a conformance pass.
A scenario can map to multiple requirements and a requirement can map to multiple scenarios without hidden aggregation.
Conflicting outcomes remain separate by implementation package, applicability, mapping version, run, cell, and evidence state.
A missing sidecar means requirement-level evidence is unavailable, not failed or unsupported.
A partial catalog cannot define a whole-RFC or whole-protocol denominator.
alternatives_considered: Embedding mappings directly in Scenario v1 was rejected for the first version because existing strict models and normalization paths could drop uncoordinated fields.
Adding requirement outcomes directly to Evidence Report v1 was rejected because the strict v1 schema rejects unknown fields and historical consumers need compatibility.
Mapping scenarios only to RFC sections was rejected because section-level success would overstate coverage of individual normative requirements.
Treating overall scenario validation as every mapped requirement passing was rejected because it lacks check-level proof.
A universal specification score was rejected because scope, roles, conditional applicability, evidence authority, and testability differ.
risks: Source locators can drift if RFC ingestion loses source-unit provenance.
Package metadata can be mistaken for observed success unless the site keeps declarations and outcomes separate.
Coverage profiles can imply certification unless scope and non-goals remain visible.
Sidecars can be misjoined to newer mappings unless digests are required and verified.
Coarse executor validation artifacts may delay directly validated mappings even when broad scenarios already pass.
